An honest, verified guide to Morocco’s web agencies — written by one of them, with full transparency about that fact.
If you’ve ever googled “best web agency in Morocco,” you’ve seen the problem: every ranking is written by a directory that’s never built a website, or by an agency that quietly puts itself at #1 and hopes you won’t notice.
This article is written by an agency too — ours is called deadLine, and yes, we’re on the list. The difference is that we tell you upfront, we put nine of our competitors on it, and every agency below was verified by hand in July 2026: websites visited, review counts checked on Sortlist, Clutch and TechBehemoths, dead domains corrected. One agency was excluded outright for selling black-hat backlink schemes. Judge the list by its usefulness, not by who wrote it.
Why international companies look at Morocco at all
Before the list, context — because if you’re reading this in English, you’re probably not in Casablanca.
Morocco has quietly become one of the strongest nearshore destinations for European companies, and a serious option for North American ones:

• Time zone: Casablanca runs on GMT/GMT+1 — the same working day as London, one hour off Paris and Berlin. Standups happen at human hours, unlike far-offshore setups.

• Languages: agencies here typically work in French, English and Arabic natively — useful if your market includes Europe, Africa or the Middle East.

• Rates: senior development capacity typically bills $25–49/hour — well below Western European rates for comparable modern-stack work (React, Next.js, Flutter, custom back-ends).

• Talent: a dense engineering-school pipeline (EMI, ENSIAS, the 1337/42 network) feeding a young, fast-maturing agency scene.
The catch: quality varies enormously, and the loudest marketing rarely correlates with the best engineering. Hence this list.
How this ranking was built
Four rules, applied to everyone including us:

• Real and verifiable — live website, Moroccan presence, recent activity. We corrected three agencies whose directory-listed domains were dead or moved.

• Third-party reviews — ratings on Sortlist, Clutch or TechBehemoths, not just testimonials on their own site.

• A clear specialty — ten interchangeable generalists help nobody; each agency here is listed for what it does best.

• Geographic spread — Casablanca dominates the market, but Rabat, Marrakech, Tangier and Agadir have serious players.
The list
1. deadLine — Casablanca. Best for: SMBs that want a website built to win clients, and custom business software.
Our agency — so instead of self-praise, here’s how to verify us in two minutes: our professional tools are public and free at deadline.ma/outils. The website audit analyzes any site’s speed, SEO, security and tech stack (it even reads .ma domain registry data — try it on your competitors). The PDF editor handles Arabic and right-to-left text entirely in the browser. An agency confident enough to publish its own engineering has nothing to hide about it. We build corporate and B2B catalogue sites, e-commerce, and custom platforms — client portals, recruitment systems, treasury tools — in French, English and Arabic, from central Casablanca.
2. Digital Nova — Casablanca. Best for: custom web/mobile apps and fast MVPs.
The #1-rated agency on Clutch Morocco (5.0 from 17 reviews). React, Next.js and Flutter, with an AI-accelerated process that ships an MVP in 6–10 weeks from roughly $7,000. If you’re a startup or a product team testing an idea, this is the profile you want.
3. Gear9 — Casablanca. Best for: enterprise and digital transformation.
A different league: Morocco’s reference Salesforce partner (Partner of the Year), with references like OCP and Attijariwafa Bank — the country’s industrial and banking giants. Custom software, CX platforms, staff augmentation. Not the right door for a $10k project; exactly the right one for a CIO.
4. V5 Digital — Casablanca. Best for: data-driven growth and acquisition.
Based at Casablanca’s Technopark, V5 combines web and app builds with genuine growth engineering — clients include Total, Siemens and OPPO. Look here when the website is one piece of a larger acquisition strategy. (Note: rebranded online — their site is now v5agency.com.)
5. FlexiApps — Rabat. Best for: iOS/Android mobile apps.
Mobile-first since 2014: React Native apps, 150+ projects delivered, apps actually live on the stores (check their portfolio — the links work, which is rarer than it should be). Sortlist 4.9 with the platform’s “trusted” badge.
6. digitransform — Rabat. Best for: SaaS platforms and engineering-heavy builds.
An engineering-culture shop (founded 2019) building SaaS products, native/hybrid apps, and data/cloud work. Sortlist 4.9, listed on both of the platform’s Morocco rankings. A solid pick when your project is a product, not a brochure.
7. MONARK IT — Marrakech. Best for: nearshore outsourcing for international clients.
Fifteen-plus years of custom web, mobile and AI development, with offices in Marrakech and Doha and an explicitly international, outsourcing-friendly setup — probably the most nearshore-ready operation on this list. Sortlist 5.0. One caution that proves our verification was real: their old .com domain is parked for sale — the live site is monarkit.net.
8. Yovista — Marrakech. Best for: full-service web + communication for SMBs.
Websites, e-commerce, social media and AI automation with a rare trait in this market: transparent, published pricing. TechBehemoths award winner three years running (2022–2024), 5.0 from 9 reviews, and a verifiable local portfolio.
9. RHILLANE — Tangier. Best for: performance marketing and paid acquisition.
The largest review footprint in Moroccan digital — 4.9 on Sortlist from over 120 reviews — with official Google, Meta and Shopify partnerships. The core skill is marketing-at-scale (SEO, Google/Meta/TikTok ads) with web builds in support. If your priority is traffic and ads rather than the product itself, this is the northern powerhouse.
10. Energiedin — Agadir. Best for: e-commerce on a controlled budget.
PrestaShop and WooCommerce specialists in Agadir, openly playing the city’s cost advantage against Casablanca and Rabat pricing — a legitimate strategy that translates into lower quotes for comparable e-commerce builds. Sortlist 5.0. Their site now lives at energiedin.com.
Quick picks by project type

• A website that generates leads for an SMB: deadLine or Yovista

• A mobile app: FlexiApps or Digital Nova

• An MVP in under 3 months: Digital Nova

• Enterprise / Salesforce: Gear9

• A SaaS product: digitransform or deadLine

• Outsourcing a dev team from Europe: MONARK IT

• E-commerce on a budget: Energiedin

• Paid acquisition at scale: RHILLANE
How to vet any Moroccan agency (or any agency, really)

• Demand a public portfolio with live links — and click them. Two agencies on early drafts of this list lost points for dead links.

• Check third-party reviews (Sortlist, Clutch, Google) — not the testimonials on their own site.

• Run their own website through an audit tool. An agency whose site is slow or unindexed is telling you something. We built a free one you can use for exactly this — no signup: deadline.ma/outils/audit-site-web.

• Insist on owning your domain and hosting. The most common horror story in this market is an agency holding a client’s domain hostage. Any serious agency registers everything in your name.

• Get the same detailed quote from two or three agencies and make them explain the differences. The explanations tell you more than the numbers.
Full disclosure, again
This list was researched and written by the team at deadLine in July 2026. We put ourselves at #1 — with our tools as the evidence, so you don’t have to take our word for anything. The other nine agencies earned their places on verifiable, third-party signals; several of them compete with us directly, and we listed them anyway, because a comparison that only contains its author is an ad, not a comparison.
Corrections welcome: if you run one of these agencies and something’s outdated, contact us via deadline.ma and we’ll fix it.
Originally researched for our French-language guide at deadline.ma — this English edition was written separately for international readers evaluating Morocco as a web development destination.
